source_paper_bibtex: @article{Berger_2025, title={Pandora’s Problem with Combinatorial Cost}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0248}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0248}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Berger, Ben and Ezra, Tomer and Feldman, Michal and Fusco, Federico}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={3161–3189} }

Section 6 (Conclusion and Future Directions), page 25.

For example, it is not clear whether there exists a poly-time algorithm that finds an optimal (or an approximately optimal) strategy under budget-additive costs.
title: Polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategies for Pandora problem with budget-additive costs
background_latex:
Pandora's problem has a ground set of boxes \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each box \(i\) contains an independent nonnegative random value \(V_i\sim D_i\) with finite expectation. A (possibly adaptive) strategy \(\pi\) sequentially chooses unopened boxes to inspect; inspecting reveals the realized value and contributes to the total inspection cost. After any inspection the strategy may halt and receive reward equal to the maximum realized value among inspected boxes.

A combinatorial inspection cost function is \(c:2^{[n]}\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), assumed normalized and monotone: \(c(\varnothing)=0\) and \(c(S)\le c(T)\) for \(S\subseteq T\). If the strategy inspects exactly the (random) set \(S(\pi)\subseteq [n]\), its expected utility is
\[
 u(\pi)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{i\in S(\pi)} V_i\Big]-\mathbb{E}[c(S(\pi))].
\]

A cost function is budget-additive (a.k.a. capped-additive) if there exist nonnegative weights \((w_i)_{i\in [n]}\) and a budget \(B\ge 0\) such that for all \(S\subseteq [n]\),
\[
 c(S)=\min\Big\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\Big\}.
\]
This class is monotone and submodular. The computational model considered in the paper represents \(c\) via oracle access (cost queries): given \(S\), a query returns \(c(S)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Design an algorithm that, given oracle access to a budget-additive cost function \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) and explicit descriptions of independent value distributions \((D_i)_{i\in[n]}\), computes either:

1) an optimal Pandora strategy \(\pi^*\in\arg\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\), or

2) a strategy \(\pi\) with provable approximation guarantee for \(\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\),

in time polynomial in \(n\) (and the input encoding / desired accuracy), using only polynomially many cost queries.
latex:
Pandora's problem has a ground set of boxes \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each box \(i\) contains an independent nonnegative random value \(V_i\sim D_i\) with finite expectation. A (possibly adaptive) strategy \(\pi\) sequentially chooses unopened boxes to inspect; inspecting reveals the realized value and contributes to the total inspection cost. After any inspection the strategy may halt and receive reward equal to the maximum realized value among inspected boxes.

A combinatorial inspection cost function is \(c:2^{[n]}\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), assumed normalized and monotone: \(c(\varnothing)=0\) and \(c(S)\le c(T)\) for \(S\subseteq T\). If the strategy inspects exactly the (random) set \(S(\pi)\subseteq [n]\), its expected utility is
\[
 u(\pi)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{i\in S(\pi)} V_i\Big]-\mathbb{E}[c(S(\pi))].
\]

A cost function is budget-additive (a.k.a. capped-additive) if there exist nonnegative weights \((w_i)_{i\in [n]}\) and a budget \(B\ge 0\) such that for all \(S\subseteq [n]\),
\[
 c(S)=\min\Big\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\Big\}.
\]
This class is monotone and submodular. The computational model considered in the paper represents \(c\) via oracle access (cost queries): given \(S\), a query returns \(c(S)\).

Design an algorithm that, given oracle access to a budget-additive cost function \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) and explicit descriptions of independent value distributions \((D_i)_{i\in[n]}\), computes either:

1) an optimal Pandora strategy \(\pi^*\in\arg\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\), or

2) a strategy \(\pi\) with provable approximation guarantee for \(\max_{\pi} u(\pi)\),

in time polynomial in \(n\) (and the input encoding / desired accuracy), using only polynomially many cost queries.
open_problem_quote: For example, it is not clear whether there exists a poly-time algorithm that finds an optimal (or an approximately optimal) strategy under budget-additive costs.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existential about algorithms (efficient optimal/approximate computation), not a crisp universal inequality that can be falsified by a single finite instance. While one can experiment on instances, failure of specific heuristics does not constitute a counterexample to existence of a polynomial-time algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate strategies/algorithms (e.g., parameterized fixed-order rules, dynamic programs, or approximation heuristics) can be encoded and evaluated on simulated instances to optimize empirical utility or worst-case observed ratios. However, the target is a worst-case polynomial-time guarantee, and empirical fitness signals are only partial proxies for proving approximation ratios or exact optimality.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the source paper (Berger–Ezra–Feldman–Fusco, 2023), Pandora’s problem is extended from additive inspection costs to a general monotone set function \(c:2^{[n]}\to\mathbb R_{\ge 0}\). Two key takeaways frame the budget-additive open problem. First, for submodular costs, structural simplicity survives: there exists an optimal strategy with a non-adaptive inspection order (Theorem 4.1), obtained via a reduction to Bernoulli instances and a proof that an optimal policy can be taken to be “impulsive” (open in a fixed order and stop at the first nonzero). Second, computational simplicity fails dramatically in the value-oracle model: even deciding whether \(\max_\pi u(\pi)>0\) requires super-polynomially many cost queries (Theorem 5.2), and hence no polynomial-query approximation is possible for general submodular costs.

Budget-additive costs \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) form a prominent subclass of submodular functions with additional structure (a single global cap). The cited follow-up/related works largely advance orthogonal directions—correlated values, time/order constraints, and general costly-information acquisition frameworks—typically under additive per-box costs. The most directly relevant progress is indirect: the thesis “Data Driven Search With Costly Information: When to Open Pandora's Box” and “Approximating pandora's box with correlations” develop approximation frameworks and reductions (e.g., to Uniform Decision Tree / Min-Sum Set Cover with Feedback) for correlated Pandora variants, while “Combinatorial selection with costly information” and “Commitment gap via correlation gap” provide composition and ex ante relaxation tools for costly-information MDP models under matroid-type constraints. These techniques suggest possible routes to handle the cap \(B\) by converting it into a coupling/feasibility constraint or by designing surrogate-cost indices, but none yields a polynomial-time (or polynomial-query) algorithm for the oracle-given budget-additive set-cost model.

Consequently, the specific question posed in the source paper’s conclusion—whether budget-additive costs admit polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategies with only polynomially many cost queries—appears to remain open based on the provided forward citations. A central remaining gap is to reconcile the strong oracle lower bounds for general submodular costs with the extra parametric structure of budget-additive functions: either by exploiting that structure algorithmically (e.g., learning \(w\) and \(B\) sufficiently from queries, then optimizing a fixed-order-with-thresholds policy), or by proving matching query lower bounds even for capped-additive costs. Promising directions include: (i) reductions that treat the cap as a knapsack-like resource and apply prophet-inequality/thresholding methods developed for constrained Pandora variants, and (ii) adapting CICS water-filling and ex ante LP approaches to derive efficiently computable indices/thresholds when the only non-additivity is the global cap.

sources.title: Data Driven Search With Costly Information: When to Open Pandora's Box
sources.theorem_or_result: The dissertation develops polynomial-time constant-factor approximation algorithms for Pandora’s Box with correlated values under standard per-box opening costs (additive costs), e.g. a 9.22-approximation against the optimal partially-adaptive benchmark (Theorem 3.5) and improved greedy “generalized Weitzman” algorithms giving 4.428-approximation (Variant 1, Theorem 4.2) and 5.828-approximation (Variant 2, Theorem 4.3). It also gives poly-time \(\tilde O(\log m)\)-approximation against the fully-adaptive optimum via reductions to Uniform Decision Tree (Corollary 5.3/5.5), where \(m\) is the number of scenarios in the explicit support.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategy for Pandora’s problem when the inspection cost is budget-additive \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) given by a value oracle. This dissertation’s algorithms and reductions address (i) correlated-value Pandora’s Box with *additive per-box* opening costs (\(\sum_{i\in S} c_i\)), and (ii) extensions to other feasibility constraints (k items, matroids), but it does not treat submodular/budget-additive inspection costs given by oracle. Budget-additive costs couple the decision to open boxes through a global cap \(B\), which is structurally different from the additive-cost model used throughout the thesis. Thus it does not resolve the stated open problem; at best it provides related techniques (LP relaxations/rounding, reductions to set cover/decision trees) that might inspire approaches for oracle-given submodular costs.

sources.title: Combinatorial selection with costly information
sources.bibtex:
@inproceedings{unknown_cics_2024,
  title        = {Combinatorial selection with costly information},
  author       = {},
  year         = {\n},
  doi          = {10.1137/1.9781611978971.26},
  url          = {https://doi.org/10.1137/1.9781611978971.26},
}
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops an amortization-and-composition framework for Costly Information Combinatorial Selection (CICS), modeling each item’s information acquisition as an acyclic MDP under matroid feasibility constraints. Core results include:
- For Markov-chain components, a “water-filling” cost amortization yields surrogate costs and an index policy that is optimal for matroid-min-CICS (Theorem 3.2).
- For general acyclic MDP components, it defines water-filling surrogate costs via optimality curves and proves a Whittle-integral-type lower bound on the optimal adaptive policy: for any matroid-min-CICS instance 
\[\mathrm{OPT}(I) \ge \mathbb{E}\big[\min_{S\in F}\sum_{i\in S} W^*_{M_i}\big].\]
- If each component MDP admits an \(\alpha\)-local approximation commitment \(\pi_i\) (i.e. \(f_{M_i^{\pi_i}}(\alpha y)\le \alpha f_{M_i}(y)\) for all \(y\)), then the resulting committing policy achieves global \(\alpha\)-approximation (Theorem 4.1).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategy for Pandora’s problem with a budget-additive (capped-additive) *set-dependent* inspection cost \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) given by oracle queries.

This paper does not study set-dependent inspection costs; instead it studies *per-action/per-item* costs within independent acyclic MDPs, combined with a *feasibility constraint* (primarily matroids) on which items may be ultimately selected. Its approximation guarantees are for CICS objectives of the form “selected values + action costs” (minimization) or “selected values − action costs” (maximization), not for a utility that subtracts a submodular cost \(c(S)\) of the *entire inspected set*.

Nevertheless, the paper is relevant as a tool/framework: it provides (i) surrogate-cost amortization ideas (water filling / Whittle-integral lower bounds) and (ii) composition theorems turning per-item local approximations into global approximations under matroid (and more generally frugal) constraints. These techniques could potentially inspire approaches to budget-additive costs by viewing the cap \(B\) as a global coupling constraint, but the paper does not provide an algorithm or approximation guarantee for oracle-given budget-additive inspection costs in Pandora’s original “max of revealed values minus cost of inspected set” formulation.

sources.title: Pandora's box problem with time constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: They define the very general “Pandora’s Box Over Time” model (time-dependent costs ci(t), time-dependent reward distributions \(D_i^t\), processing times \(p_i\), and post-inspection value discounting \(\bar v_i\)) and give polynomial-time constant-factor approximation algorithms. Main guarantees include: (i) a 21.3-approximation for the general model (Theorem 3.1), obtained via a reduction to maximizing \(f(M)=\mathbb E[\max_{e\in M} Y_e]\) over matchings in a block bipartite hypergraph and a 5.32-approximation for this “Submodular Block Matching” problem (Theorem 3.7); (ii) improved \((4+\varepsilon)\)-approximations when processing times are zero / time-slot availability constraints (Theorems 4.2 and 4.4); and (iii) a 1.37-approximation for “value discounting” with time-independent costs and distributions (Theorem 4.5).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategies for Pandora’s problem with budget-additive (capped-additive) combinatorial inspection costs given by an oracle \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\). This paper does not study combinatorial set costs; its costs are additive across inspected boxes (possibly time-dependent per-box costs \(\bar c_i(t)\)) and feasibility is modeled via time/processing constraints rather than a submodular cost oracle. Thus it does not resolve the budget-additive-cost Pandora open problem. However, it contributes relevant techniques—reductions to constrained Pandora instances, adaptivity-gap arguments, prophet-inequality-based stopping rules, and especially approximation algorithms for maximizing \(\mathbb E[\max_{e\in M} Y_e]\) under structured combinatorial constraints (block bipartite hypergraph matchings). These tools may be adaptable to designing approximation algorithms when the inspection “cost” is submodular/budget-additive, but no such result is claimed here.

sources.title: Commitment gap via correlation gap
sources.theorem_or_result: For Costly Information Combinatorial Selection (CICS) with feasibility constraint \(F\), the paper relates the best achievable performance of committing policies to ex ante free-order prophet inequalities for \(F\). In particular it proves (Corollary 5.5) that for any CICS instance \(I=(F,M)\), the commitment gap satisfies \(\mathrm{ComGap}(I)\ge \alpha_F\), where \(\alpha_F:=\min_D \mathrm{FreeOrder}(F,D)/\mathrm{ExAnte}(F,D)\) (equivalently the frugal correlation gap). It also shows (Lemma 3.1 + Section 6) that the ex ante optimal CICS policy can be computed exactly and efficiently (via an occupation-measure LP) and yields an optimal commitment \(P\) with \(\mathrm{ExAnteOpt}(I)=\mathrm{ExAnteOpt}(I^P)\). For matroid \(F\), this yields an efficient \((1-1/e)\)-approximation by committing policies (Table 1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for polynomial-time optimal or approximation algorithms for Pandora’s problem with budget-additive (capped-additive) inspection costs given by an oracle. This paper does not study combinatorial inspection cost functions \(c(S)\) of the form \(\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\); instead it studies CICS where costs arise from per-element probing MDPs and feasibility is a downward-closed constraint \(F\) on the finally selected set. Its main contribution is a general approximation framework for CICS via ex ante relaxations, surrogate values, and (ex ante) free-order prophet inequalities, plus exact computation of the ex ante optimum under a separation oracle for \(P(F)\). These techniques may be conceptually relevant if one can reduce budget-additive inspection costs to a CICS/MDP model or otherwise connect the Pandora-with-submodular-cost objective to an ex ante relaxation and prophet-inequality rounding, but the paper does not provide such a reduction or an algorithm for budget-additive cost Pandora directly.

sources.title: Delegation with costly inspection
sources.bibtex:
@inproceedings{unknown_delegation_costly_inspection,
  title        = {Delegation with costly inspection},
  author       = {},
  year         = {\n},
  doi          = {10.1145/3736252.3742640},
  url          = {https://doi.org/10.1145/3736252.3742640},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces Delegated Choice with Inspection Cost (DCIC), a generalization of Pandora’s box with nonobligatory inspection (PNOI), and proves constant-factor approximation mechanisms in several regimes. In particular, for costless delegation (Del=0) it gives a 3-approximation mechanism (Theorem 4.8) and shows this factor is tight for that mechanism (Theorem 4.11). It also extends the 3-approximation analysis to arbitrary monotone set-function inspection costs (Corollary 4.9).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategies for Pandora’s problem with budget-additive (capped-additive) combinatorial costs given by oracle access. This paper does not study that model: its main setting is DCIC with a strategic agent and (mostly) additive per-item inspection costs, with an extension to arbitrary monotone set-function costs in a specific costless-delegation regime. While Corollary 4.9 mentions monotone combinatorial costs, the mechanism there uses at most one inspection and the analysis upper-bounds costs via singleton costs, so it does not yield an approximation algorithm for Pandora’s problem with budget-additive costs (without delegation) nor address oracle-reconstruction of weights/budget. Thus it does not resolve the open problem, but provides related conceptual/technical ideas about handling monotone set-function costs and approximation via simple max-of-two mechanisms.

sources.title: Optimal 4-Approximation for the Correlated Pandora's Problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Main theorem (Theorem 15): For the Correlated Pandora’s Problem in the minimization form (minimize total opening cost plus the minimum revealed volume), the authors give a polynomial-time 4-approximation among partially adaptive policies (fixed opening order, adaptive stopping). The algorithm is Poisson rounding of a convex-program relaxation plus a “Balanced Stopping” rule, achieving expected objective at most \(4\cdot \mathrm{OPT}_{\text{part-adapt}}\).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for polynomial-time optimal or approximate strategies for the *classical* (independent values) Pandora problem with a *budget-additive set function* inspection cost \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) given by oracle, and objective \(\mathbb E[\max V_i]-\mathbb E[c(S)]\) (a maximization with a submodular cost).

This citing paper studies a different model: Correlated Pandora’s Problem with *additive per-box costs* \(c_i\) and a *minimization* objective (cost + min volume), and it restricts to partially adaptive policies. Its 4-approximation does not address budget-additive (capped-additive) *set-function* costs, nor the maximization objective with independent values. However, it contributes relevant techniques—LP/convex relaxations, Poisson rounding, and stopping-rule analysis—that might inspire approximation algorithms for other Pandora variants with non-additive costs.

sources.title: Approximating pandora's box with correlations
sources.bibtex:
@article{unknown_approximating_pandoras_box_correlations,
  title        = {Approximating pandora's box with correlations},
  author       = {},
  year         = {\n},
  eprint       = {2108.12976},
  archivePrefix= {arXiv},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper establishes approximation-preserving reductions showing that Pandora’s Box (PB) with correlated values is equivalent (up to polylog factors) to Uniform Decision Tree (UDT) and to a new Min-Sum Set Cover with Feedback (MSSCf). In particular, it gives a polynomial-time \(\tilde O(\log m)\)-approximation for PB when the joint distribution has explicit support of size \(m\) (Corollary 3.1/3.3), and under a separability condition for a mixture of \(m\) product distributions it gives a constant-factor approximation running in time \(n^{\tilde O(m^2/\varepsilon^2)}\) (Corollary 6.1, via Theorem 6.1 for \(\mathrm{PB}_{\le T}\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for polynomial-time optimal or approximation algorithms for Pandora’s problem with budget-additive *combinatorial inspection costs* \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\) given by a value oracle. This citing paper studies a different axis: *correlations* in box values under (mostly) additive per-box opening costs (unit costs, with an extension to non-unit additive costs). It does not model or address submodular/budget-additive set-dependent costs, nor oracle access to \(c\). However, its reductions to decision-tree/cover problems and its thresholding framework for stopping rules could be conceptually useful as tools if one later extends them to combinatorial-cost probing/search settings.

sources.title: Pandora's problem with deadlines
sources.theorem_or_result: They study Pandora’s problem with per-box additive inspection costs and additional feasibility constraints on the inspection order (deadlines/time slots). Main guarantee: for any \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), there is a polynomial-time threshold strategy achieving an \((\tfrac{e-1}{4e}-\varepsilon)\)-approximation to the optimal expected utility for the Time Slots model (Theorem 1), and hence the same for the Deadlines model (Corollary 1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for polynomial-time optimal/approximate strategies for Pandora’s problem with budget-additive (capped-additive) combinatorial inspection costs given by oracle \(c(S)=\min\{B,\sum_{i\in S} w_i\}\). This paper does not address combinatorial costs: it assumes standard additive per-box costs \(\sum_{i\in S} c_i\) and instead adds order constraints (deadlines/time slots). However, its technique—reducing feasibility of inspectable sets to a transversal matroid and then using monotone submodular maximization plus adaptivity-gap and prophet-inequality thresholding—may be conceptually relevant if one can model budget-additive costs or the induced objective within a similar submodular/matroid framework. As written, it neither provides an algorithm nor approximation guarantee for budget-additive cost oracles.
